sourceQuestion:
Let G = (V, E) be an undirected and unweighted graph with n vertices. Let d(u, v) denote the number of edges in a shortest path between vertices u and v in G. Let the maximum value of d(u, v) such that d(u, v) <= 15 be D. Let T be any breadth-first-search tree of G. Which ONE of the given options is CORRECT for every such graph G?

A. The height of T is exactly 15.
B. The height of T is exactly D.
C. The height of T is at most 30.
D. The height of T is at least 30.
sourceAnswer: C. The height of T is at most 30.

parsed.stem: Let G = (V, E) be an undirected and unweighted graph with n vertices. Let d(u, v) denote the number of edges in a shortest path between vertices u and v in G. Let the maximum value of d(u, v) such that d(u, v) <= 15 be D. Let T be any breadth-first-search tree of G. Which ONE of the given options is CORRECT for every such graph G?

options.text: The height of T is exactly 15.
The height of T is exactly D.
The height of T is at most 30.
The height of T is at least 30.

sourceQuestion:
Let G be any undirected graph with positive edge weights, and T be a minimum spanning tree of G. For any two vertices u and v, let d_G(u, v) and d_T(u, v) be the shortest distances between u and v in G and T, respectively. Which ONE of the options is CORRECT for all possible G, T, u, and v?

A. d_G(u, v) = d_T(u, v)
B. d_G(u, v) <= d_T(u, v)
C. d_G(u, v) >= d_T(u, v)
D. d_G(u, v) > d_T(u, v)

parsed.stem: Let G be any undirected graph with positive edge weights, and T be a minimum spanning tree of G. For any two vertices u and v, let d_G(u, v) and d_T(u, v) be the shortest distances between u and v in G and T, respectively. Which ONE of the options is CORRECT for all possible G, T, u, and v?

sourceQuestion:
Let G be an edge-weighted undirected graph with positive edge weights. Suppose a positive constant c is added to the weight of every edge. Which ONE of the following statements is TRUE about the minimum spanning trees (MSTs) and shortest paths (SPs) in G before and after the edge weight update?

A. Every MST remains an MST, and every SP remains an SP.
B. MSTs need not remain MSTs, and every SP remains an SP.
C. Every MST remains an MST, and SPs need not remain SPs.
D. MSTs need not remain MSTs, and SPs need not remain SPs.
sourceAnswer: C. Every MST remains an MST, and SPs need not remain SPs.

parsed.stem: Let G be an edge-weighted undirected graph with positive edge weights. Suppose a positive constant c is added to the weight of every edge. Which ONE of the following statements is TRUE about the minimum spanning trees (MSTs) and shortest paths (SPs) in G before and after the edge weight update?

options.text: Every MST remains an MST, and every SP remains an SP.
MSTs need not remain MSTs, and every SP remains an SP.
Every MST remains an MST, and SPs need not remain SPs.
MSTs need not remain MSTs, and SPs need not remain SPs.

sourceQuestion:
Let G be a simple, unweighted, and undirected graph. A subset of the vertices and edges of G are shown below. It is given that a -> b -> c -> d is a shortest path between a and d; e -> f -> g -> h is a shortest path between e and h; and b -> c -> a -> e is a shortest path between b and e.

The graph shows vertices a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h interconnected.

Which of the following is/are NOT the edges of G?

A. (b, d)
B. (b, f)
C. (b, h)
D. (r, g)

The graph shows vertices a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h interconnected.

Which of the following is/are NOT the edges of G?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a weighted, undirected graph with positive edge weights and let (u, v) be an edge in the graph. It is known that the shortest path from the source vertex s to u has weight 53 and the shortest path from s to v has weight 65. Which one of the following statements is always TRUE?

A. Weight(u, v) <= 12
B. Weight(u, v) <= 12
C. Weight(u, v) >= 12
D. Weight(u, v) > 12

B. Quick sort
C. Merge sort

sourceQuestion:
A two-dimensional array A[1..n][1..n] of integers is partially sorted if A[i][j] < A[i][j+1] and A[i][j] < A[i+1][j] for all 1 <= i, j <= n-1.

a. The smallest item in the array is at A[i][j] where i = ___ and j = ___.

b. The smallest item is deleted. Complete the following procedure to insert item x (which is guaranteed to be smaller than any item in the last row or column) still keeping A partially sorted.

procedure insert(x: integer);
var i, j: integer;
begin
  A[1][1] := x; i := 1; j := 1;
  repeat
    [S]
  until A[i][j] = x
end;

A. S: if A[i][j+1] < A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end
B. S: if A[i][j+1] > A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end
C. S: if A[i][j+1] < A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end
D. S: if A[i][j+1] > A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end
sourceAnswer: A. S: if A[i][j+1] < A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end

options.text: S: if A[i][j+1] < A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end
S: if A[i][j+1] > A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end
S: if A[i][j+1] < A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end
S: if A[i][j+1] > A[i+1][j] then begin swap(A[i][j], A[i+1][j]); i := i+1 end else begin swap(A[i][j], A[i][j+1]); j := j+1 end

sourceQuestion:
A sorting technique is called stable if

A. it takes O(n log n) time
B. it maintains the relative order of occurrence of non-distinct elements
C. it uses divide and conquer paradigm
D. it takes O(n) space
sourceAnswer: B. it maintains the relative order of occurrence of non-distinct elements

parsed.stem: A sorting technique is called stable if

options.text: it takes O(n log n) time
it maintains the relative order of occurrence of non-distinct elements
it uses divide and conquer paradigm

sourceQuestion:
In a permutation a_1, a_2, ..., a_n of n distinct integers, an inversion is a pair (a_i, a_j) such that i < j and a_i > a_j. If all permutations are equally likely, what is the expected number of inversions in a randomly chosen permutation of {1, 2, ..., n}?

A. n(n-1)/2
B. n(n-1)/4
C. n(n+1)/4
D. 2n[log_2(n)]

parsed.stem: In a permutation a_1, a_2, ..., a_n of n distinct integers, an inversion is a pair (a_i, a_j) such that i < j and a_i > a_j. If all permutations are equally likely, what is the expected number of inversions in a randomly chosen permutation of {1, 2, ..., n}?

sourceQuestion:
Let a and b be two sorted arrays each containing n integers in non-decreasing order. Let c be a sorted array containing 2n integers obtained by merging the two arrays a and b. Assuming the arrays are indexed starting from 1, consider the following four statements:

I.   a[n/2] >= c[n]
II.  a[n/2] <= c[n]
III. b[n] = c[2n]
IV.  a[n] = c[2n-1] or a[n] = c[2n]

Which of the following is TRUE?

A. only I and II
B. only I and IV
C. only II and III
D. only III and IV

II.  a[n/2] <= c[n]

A. Theta(n)

C. Theta(m+n)
D. Theta(mn)

sourceAnswer: A. The program implements minimax search; alpha-beta pruning can reduce search
